sn47.6:1.1: “Once a hawk suddenly swooped down on a quail and seized it.
sn47.6:1.2: Then the quail, as it was being carried off by the hawk, lamented,
sn47.6:1.3: ‘O, just my bad luck and lack of merit that I was wandering out of my proper range and into the territory of others!
sn47.6:1.4: If only I had kept to my proper range today, to my own ancestral territory, this hawk would have been no match for me in battle.’
sn47.6:1.5: “‘But what is your proper range?’ the hawk asked. ‘What is your own ancestral territory?’
sn47.6:1.6: “‘A newly plowed field with clumps of earth all turned up.’
sn47.6:1.7: “So the hawk, without bragging about its own strength, without mentioning its own strength, let go of the quail.
sn47.6:1.8: ‘Go, quail, but even when you have gone there you won’t escape me.’
sn47.6:2.1: “Then the quail, having gone to a newly plowed field with clumps of earth all turned up and climbing up on top of a large clump of earth, stood taunting the hawk, ‘Now come and get me, you hawk!
sn47.6:2.2: Now come and get me, you hawk!’
sn47.6:2.3: “So the hawk, without bragging about its own strength, without mentioning its own strength, folded its two wings and suddenly swooped down toward the quail.
sn47.6:2.4: When the quail knew, ‘The hawk is coming at me full speed,’ it slipped behind the clump of earth,
sn47.6:2.5: and right there the hawk shattered its own breast.
sn47.6:2.6: “This is what happens to anyone who wanders into what is not his proper range and is the territory of others.
sn47.6:3.1: “For this reason, you should not wander into what is not your proper range and is the territory of others.
sn47.6:3.2: In one who wanders into what is not his proper range and is the territory of others, Māra gains an opening, Māra gains a foothold.
sn47.6:3.3: And what, for a monk, is not his proper range and is the territory of others?
sn47.6:3.6: Forms cognizable by the eye—agreeable, pleasing, charming, endearing, enticing, linked to sensual desire.
sn47.6:3.10: Tactile sensations cognizable by the body—agreeable, pleasing, charming, endearing, enticing, linked to sensual desire.
sn47.6:3.11: These, for a monk, are not his proper range and are the territory of others.
sn47.6:4.1: “Wander, monks, in what is your proper range, your own ancestral territory.
sn47.6:4.2: In one who wanders in what is his proper range, his own ancestral territory, Māra gains no opening, Māra gains no foothold.
sn47.6:4.3: And what, for a monk, is his proper range, his own ancestral territory?
sn47.6:4.7: There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.6:4.8: He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves…
sn47.6:4.10: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.6:4.11: This, for a monk, is his proper range, his own ancestral territory.”
